title: PacketForge Lambda Lockout: The Missing Permission

narrator.personality: Senior platform engineer. Direct and technical. Frustrated because this was supposed to be a routine deployment. Keeps checking the customer dashboard for churn signals.

story_beats.message: Product team confirms: the threat-feed sync has not run since the deployment. Enterprise customers on the Sentinel plan are seeing stale threat intelligence data. Three of them have SLAs that guarantee updates within 15 minutes.

Customer success just flagged that Ridgeline Financial -- our largest enterprise account, $480K ARR -- opened a P1 support ticket. Their security operations center is relying on our threat feeds for real-time blocking rules.
The Lambda function code is correct -- it worked fine in staging. The difference has to be in the execution environment, not the code. What permissions does the function have in production?
The Lambda function is invoking correctly and the DynamoDB table exists. The issue is not connectivity or code -- it is authorization. What does the execution role allow this function to do?

hints.text: The error message says AccessDeniedException. What controls whether a Lambda function can access other AWS services?
Lambda functions use an IAM execution role to interact with other AWS services. Have you looked at the permissions attached to this function's role?
The execution role has DynamoDB permissions, but check the specific actions. Does it allow the operations the function is trying to perform?
The role allows dynamodb:GetItem and dynamodb:Query, but the function also needs dynamodb:PutItem and dynamodb:UpdateItem to write threat feed data. Those actions are missing from the policy.

glossary.Execution Role: An IAM role assigned to a Lambda function that defines which AWS services and resources the function is allowed to access. Without it, the function cannot call any other AWS API.
glossary.IAM Policy: A JSON document that lists specific actions (like dynamodb:PutItem) a user or role is allowed or denied. Policies are attached to roles or users to grant permissions.
glossary.Least Privilege: A security principle where you grant only the exact permissions needed and nothing more. If a permission is not explicitly allowed, it is denied by default.
glossary.AccessDeniedException: An error returned by AWS when a request tries to perform an action that the caller's IAM policy does not allow. It means the permission is missing, not that the resource is gone.
glossary.Trust Policy: A special IAM policy on a role that says which service (like lambda.amazonaws.com) is allowed to assume that role. It does not grant permissions to AWS resources directly.
glossary.DynamoDB Action: A specific operation you can perform on a DynamoDB table, such as GetItem (read one row), PutItem (write one row), or Query (search by key). Each action must be separately allowed in an IAM policy.
glossary.ARN: Amazon Resource Name. A unique identifier for any AWS resource, such as a specific DynamoDB table or IAM role. Policies use ARNs to scope permissions to exact resources.
glossary.CloudWatch Logs: A service that collects and stores log output from Lambda functions and other AWS services. Error messages like AccessDeniedException appear here.
glossary.BatchWriteItem: A DynamoDB operation that writes up to 25 items in a single API call. It is a different action from PutItem and requires its own IAM permission.

narrative_arc.call: CloudWatch error rate dashboard turns red within minutes of a routine deployment. Every Lambda invocation fails with AccessDeniedException.
narrative_arc.threshold: The player opens CloudWatch logs and sees the AccessDeniedException errors. The function fetches data successfully but fails on every write to DynamoDB.
narrative_arc.trials: The Lambda code is correct and worked in staging. The DynamoDB table exists and is healthy. The function invokes on schedule. The red herring is assuming the problem is in the code, the table, or network connectivity.
narrative_arc.revelation: The IAM execution role only allows read actions (GetItem, Query) and the now-unused BatchWriteItem. The refactored code uses PutItem and UpdateItem, which were never added to the policy.
narrative_arc.return: IAM policies must be updated whenever code changes introduce new API calls. Staging environments should mirror production IAM policies. A policy simulation step in CI/CD prevents this class of failure.

components.name: packetforge-threat-sync (Lambda function)
components.role: Pulls threat data from 14 external feeds every 5 minutes, enriches it, and writes results to DynamoDB.
connections: Triggered by EventBridge rule on 5-minute schedule
Reads and writes to packetforge-threats DynamoDB table
components.failure_impact: If this function fails, threat intelligence data goes stale and enterprise customers receive outdated threat feeds.

components.name: packetforge-threat-sync-role (IAM execution role)
components.role: Grants the Lambda function permission to call specific AWS APIs. Defines what the function is allowed to do.
connections: Policy scoped to packetforge-threats DynamoDB table and CloudWatch Logs
components.failure_impact: If the role's policy is missing required actions, the function receives AccessDeniedException on every disallowed API call.

components.name: packetforge-threats (DynamoDB table)
components.role: Stores enriched threat intelligence data. Serves as the backing store for the customer-facing API.
connections: Written to by packetforge-threat-sync Lambda function
Read by the customer API running on ECS Fargate
components.failure_impact: If writes fail, the table serves stale data. The table itself remains healthy -- the problem is upstream authorization.

components.name: CloudWatch Logs and Metrics
components.role: Captures Lambda invocation logs and error metrics. The error rate alarm fires when invocations fail.
connections: Receives logs from packetforge-threat-sync Lambda function
Error alarm triggers on non-zero error count
components.failure_impact: If logging permissions were also missing, the team would have no visibility into why the function fails.

system_narration.data_flow: EventBridge schedule -> Lambda function fetches from external threat feeds -> Lambda enriches data -> Lambda writes to DynamoDB table -> Customer API reads from DynamoDB -> Enterprise customers
system_narration.what_broke: The IAM execution role's policy allowed GetItem, Query, and BatchWriteItem. The code refactor replaced BatchWriteItem with PutItem and UpdateItem, but nobody updated the IAM policy. Production's least-privilege role correctly denied the unauthorized actions.

resolution.root_cause: The Lambda function packetforge-threat-sync's IAM execution role only grants dynamodb:GetItem and dynamodb:Query permissions. After a code refactor, the function now also requires dynamodb:PutItem and dynamodb:UpdateItem to write threat feed data to DynamoDB, but these actions were never added to the execution role policy.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the Lambda execution role is missing dynamodb:PutItem and dynamodb:UpdateItem permissions
Player connects the issue to the recent code deployment that added write operations not covered by the existing IAM policy
Player proposes adding the missing DynamoDB write permissions to the execution role's IAM policy

learning_objectives: Lambda functions use IAM execution roles to interact with other AWS services -- the role's policies define what the function can do
IAM policies follow the principle of least privilege -- only the explicitly allowed actions are permitted
When deploying code changes that interact with new AWS API actions, the execution role must be updated to match
sop_steps: Check CloudWatch Logs for the failing Lambda function to identify the exact error message and which API action was denied.
Extract the IAM execution role ARN from the Lambda function configuration using get_function_configuration.
Retrieve the IAM policies attached to the execution role using list_attached_role_policies and get_role_policy.
Compare the allowed actions in the policy against the actions the function code actually calls (PutItem, UpdateItem, GetItem, Query).
Identify the missing actions: dynamodb:PutItem and dynamodb:UpdateItem are not in the policy.
Update the IAM policy to add the missing actions scoped to the specific DynamoDB table ARN (arn:aws:dynamodb:us-east-1:672913845210:table/packetforge-threats).
Remove dynamodb:BatchWriteItem from the policy since the refactored code no longer uses it.
Wait for the next scheduled invocation (or invoke manually) and confirm the function succeeds in CloudWatch Logs.
Verify the DynamoDB write count metric returns to a non-zero value in CloudWatch Metrics.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Lambda function has the correct IAM permissions but the DynamoDB table uses a KMS encryption key the execution role cannot access.
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The error would be AccessDeniedException on kms:Decrypt or kms:GenerateDataKey rather than on the DynamoDB action itself. The IAM policy for DynamoDB looks correct, but a separate KMS key policy blocks access.
related_failure_modes.prevention: When a DynamoDB table uses a customer-managed KMS key, include kms:Decrypt and kms:GenerateDataKey permissions in the execution role, scoped to the key ARN.

related_failure_modes.scenario: An SCP (Service Control Policy) at the AWS Organization level denies DynamoDB write actions in the production account.
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The IAM execution role policy would look correct with the right actions allowed, but the SCP overrides it at the organization level. The deny comes from above the account, not from the role itself.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Audit SCPs in AWS Organizations before deploying. Use iam:SimulatePrincipalPolicy with the production role to test effective permissions before deployment.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The Lambda function's resource-based policy restricts which principals can invoke it, and EventBridge loses invoke permission after a role change.
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The function would never execute at all -- CloudWatch Logs would show no invocation, not an AccessDeniedException during execution. The problem is invocation authorization, not execution authorization.
related_failure_modes.prevention: After changing Lambda resource policies, test that the event source (EventBridge, S3, etc.) can still invoke the function by triggering a test event.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The IAM policy uses a condition key (e.g., aws:SourceVpc) that does not match the Lambda function's execution environment.
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The actions would be listed as allowed in the policy, but a condition restricts when they apply. The deny is conditional, not a missing action. SimulatePrincipalPolicy without context keys would show Allow, masking the real issue.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Avoid VPC or IP-based condition keys on Lambda execution roles unless the function runs in a VPC. Test policies with SimulatePrincipalPolicy using realistic context keys.

sop_practices: Run iam:SimulatePrincipalPolicy against the production execution role for every DynamoDB action the code uses before deploying. Add this as a CI/CD pipeline gate.
Use identical least-privilege IAM policies in staging and production. Never use wildcard permissions (dynamodb:*) in staging to avoid masking permission gaps.
When refactoring code that changes which AWS API actions are called, treat the IAM policy as part of the code change -- update and review it in the same pull request.
Set a CloudWatch alarm on the Lambda Errors metric with a threshold of 1 and a 1-minute evaluation period so permission failures are caught within one invocation cycle.
